content: Systems engineering V-model: Requirements → Architecture → Design → Implementation → Unit Test → Integration Test → System Test → Acceptance. Left side decomposes, right side verifies.
Interface Control Documents (ICDs) define the contract between subsystems. Every interface should have an ICD before implementation begins.
Conway's Law: system architecture mirrors the communication structure of the organization that builds it. Design the org structure you want the architecture to reflect.

SOLID principles apply beyond OOP: Single Responsibility (one reason to change), Open-Closed (extend not modify), Liskov Substitution (subtypes are substitutable), Interface Segregation (narrow interfaces), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions).

Fallacies of distributed computing: the network is reliable, latency is zero, bandwidth is infinite, the network is secure, topology doesn't change, there is one administrator, transport cost is zero, the network is homogeneous.
Two Generals Problem: in an unreliable network, no protocol can guarantee that two parties agree on a shared state. All distributed consensus is approximate.
CAP theorem: a distributed system can provide at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance simultaneously. In practice, P is non-negotiable, so the real choice is CP vs AP.
Amdahl's Law: the speedup from parallelization is limited by the sequential fraction. If 10% of work is sequential, max speedup is 10x regardless of processor count.

Technical debt taxonomy: deliberate-prudent (we know, will fix later), deliberate-reckless (we know, won't fix), inadvertent-prudent (learned better approach), inadvertent-reckless (didn't know we were making debt).
Observability pillars: metrics (what is happening), logs (what happened), traces (how it happened across services). All three are needed; none alone is sufficient.
